description: This figure comprises two subplots showing the temperature dependence of compression yield stress for Fe-26Al-based alloys from room temperature to 800°C. Subplot (a) presents data for binary Fe-26Al (open squares) compared with Ti- and V-containing alloys: Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C (diamonds), Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C (circles), and Fe-26Al-2V-1C (triangles). Subplot (b) shows the same baseline Fe-26Al alongside Nb- and Ta-containing alloys: Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C (triangles), Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C (asterisks), and Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C (crosses). All Fe-26Al-based alloys exhibit an anomalous yield strength increase at intermediate temperatures, peaking around 550°C at approximately 400 MPa for Ti- and V-containing alloys in (a). In (b), the yield stress anomaly peaks for Nb- and Ta-containing alloys shift toward lower temperatures and higher stresses compared to the binary alloy, with Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C showing the most significant shift to 474°C with a peak stress of 619 MPa. The curves demonstrate that precipitation strengthening via MC carbides and Laves phase (Fe,Al)₂Nb effectively enhances high-temperature strength, with the Laves phase providing superior strengthening at elevated temperatures due to higher thermal stability.
